perturbation.original_text: No later than [***] ([***]) days following the Effective Date, Ginkgo will pay to BLI a non-refundable upfront amount equal to Ten Million Dollars ($10,000,000) (the "Upfront Payment"). Such amount will be fully creditable against all Development Purchases and Production Purchases owed by Ginkgo to BLI [***] and will be fully creditable against the Full Purchase Target and, [***], the Minimum Purchase Commitment, Development Purchase Commitment and Production Purchase Target [***]. [***].
perturbation.changed_text: No later than 90 days following the Effective Date, Ginkgo will pay to BLI a non-refundable upfront amount equal to Ten Million Dollars ($10,000,000) (the "Upfront Payment"). Such amount will be fully creditable against all Development Purchases and Production Purchases owed by Ginkgo to BLI [***] and will be fully creditable against the Full Purchase Target and, [***], the Minimum Purchase Commitment, Development Purchase Commitment and Production Purchase Target [***]. [***].
perturbation.explanation: The original text has a timeframe that is redacted. Changing the timeframe to 90 days may be non-compliant.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section stipulates how to compute time periods, stating that if the last day for performance falls on a holiday, the time is extended to the next business day. Specifying '90 days' without accounting for potential holidays might violate this provision if the payment due date lands on a holiday, effectively shortening the legally required payment window.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 12a. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PRELIMINARY PROVISIONS ( Preliminary Provisions enacted 1872. ) 12a. (a) If the last day for the performance of any act provided or required by law to be performed within a specified period of time is a holiday, then that period is hereby extended to and including the next day that is not a holiday. For purposes of this section, “holiday” means all day on Saturdays, all holidays specified in Section 135 and, to the extent provided in Section 12b, all days that by terms of Section 12b are required to be considered as holidays. (b) This section applies to Sections 659, 659a, and 921, and to all other provisions of law providing or requiring an act to be performed on a particular day or within a specified period of time, whether expressed in this or any other code or statute, ordinance, rule, or regulation. (Amended by Stats. 2007, Ch. 263, Sec. 3. Effective January 1, 2008.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text specifies a fixed '90 days' for payment, which could conflict with CCP 12a if the 90th day falls on a holiday. CCP 12a explicitly states, 'If the last day for the performance of any act provided or required by law...is a holiday, then that period is hereby extended to and including the next day that is not a holiday.' By setting a firm 90-day deadline without this allowance, the contract creates a potential violation of state law regarding performance deadlines, as it doesn't account for the possibility of the due date landing on a holiday, potentially shortening the legally allowed payment window.

perturbation.original_text: The Affected Party shall give prompt written notice to the other Party of such cause and shall take whatever reasonable steps are appropriate in the other Party's discretion to relieve the effect of such cause as rapidly as possible.
perturbation.changed_text: The Affected Party shall give written notice to the other Party of such cause within 14 days and shall take whatever reasonable steps are appropriate in the other Party's discretion to relieve the effect of such cause as rapidly as possible.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "prompt" to "14 days" introduces a fixed timeframe, potentially conflicting with legal expectations for timely notification in force majeure situations, which often require immediate or near-immediate notice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principle: Timeliness in Force Majeure
perturbation.law_citation: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a specific statute, the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance of their contractual obligations. A 14-day notice period may be deemed unreasonable in situations where immediate notification is feasible and necessary to mitigate damages or allow the other party to prepare for the impact of the force majeure event. 'Prompt' is subjective but generally implies a higher standard of immediacy than a fixed 14-day window, potentially conflicting with legal interpretations of reasonable action during unforeseen events.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'prompt' written notice, while the changed text specifies 'within 14 days'. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that 'prompt' generally implies a higher standard of immediacy than a fixed 14-day window. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'good faith' as encompassing honest dealing and observance of fair dealing standards. By setting a 14-day window, the changed text potentially violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, especially if a quicker notification is feasible and necessary to mitigate damages, thus creating a legal contradiction as it makes the contract unenforceable under certain circumstances. The introduction of a fixed timeframe conflicts with the standard of reasonable action during unforeseen events, per the explanations provided, and the legal definition of good faith as acting honestly.

perturbation.original_text: The types of insurance, and minimum limits will be: (i) any insurance policy that is required by any Applicable Law, including [***] and [***] policies where applicable; and (ii) [***] insurance with a minimum limit of [***] Dollars ($[***]) per occurrence and [***] Dollars ($[***]) in the aggregate.
perturbation.changed_text: The types of insurance, and minimum limits will be: (i) any insurance policy that is required by any Applicable Law, including [***] and [***] policies where applicable; and (ii) [***] insurance with a minimum limit of $1 Dollar ($1) per occurrence and $1 Dollar ($1) in the aggregate.
perturbation.explanation: Setting the insurance limit to $1 is far below what is needed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While the agreement states that each party will obtain insurance, setting it to $1 is not really holding up that requirement and can lead to legal action.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text sets the insurance limit to $1, which effectively negates the requirement for insurance as stated in the original text and explained in the 'Perturbation Explanation'. This creates an inconsistency because the 'Law Explanation' suggests that not upholding the insurance requirement can lead to legal action. While the scraped snippet discusses 'good faith' and 'honest dealing', it does not explicitly define minimum insurance limits or directly prohibit setting insurance limits to a nominal amount. However, setting the insurance limit to $1 could be seen as a failure to act in 'good faith' because it does not provide any real protection, thus contradicting the original intent of having insurance.
